metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for endoscopic submucosal dissection of gastric high-grade dysplasia; antiplatelet/bleeding-risk clearance

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: MedicationRequest aspirin 81 mg = active, annotated 'primary prevention, no CAD'
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms he has never had a heart attack, stent, or bypass; PCP started low-dose aspirin a few years ago 'just in case, for the heart'
planted_discrepancies.note: Load-bearing confirmation: aspirin is primary prevention only, so it can be held per high-bleed-risk resection protocol without cardiology clearance. No secondary-prevention indication exists.

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no anticoagulant or antiplatelet other than aspirin on file
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient volunteers he is 'on a blood thinner' — on clarification this is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3) he takes daily, not a prescription anticoagulant
planted_discrepancies.note: 'Blood thinner' = fish oil, not a true anticoagulant; no DOAC/warfarin exists. Still a mild bleeding-risk supplement to hold pre-ESD, but does NOT require anticoagulation bridging or anesthesia anticoag clearance.

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient takes over-the-counter ibuprofen 400 mg most days for right knee osteoarthritis
planted_discrepancies.note: Undocumented NSAID adds bleeding and mucosal-injury risk for a high-bleed-risk gastric resection; must be held pre-procedure.

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient takes amlodipine 5 mg every morning for blood pressure
planted_discrepancies.note: Chart medication list is incomplete; amlodipine should be captured and continued (day-of-procedure BP management).

patient reports no known drug allergies

prior_notes.text: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy performed for evaluation of intermittent epigastric discomfort. Findings: a single flat, slightly depressed 25 mm lesion in the gastric antrum with abnormal surface pattern on narrow-band imaging; margins marked. Biopsies obtained. Remainder of exam unremarkable. No active bleeding. Plan: await pathology; discuss endoscopic resection if dysplasia confirmed. Patient on low-dose aspirin (primary prevention) — no other antithrombotic reported at time of exam.

Follow-up to review antral biopsy showing high-grade dysplasia. Staging endoscopic ultrasound performed same week demonstrated a mucosally-confined lesion with intact submucosal layer and no evidence of submucosal invasion or suspicious perigastric lymphadenopathy — favorable for en-bloc endoscopic resection. Plan: refer for endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD). Advised pre-procedure review of antithrombotic and bleeding-risk medications. Hypertension noted; continue home regimen.

code.text: Gastric antral adenoma with high-grade dysplasia (disorder)

note.text: Primary prevention; no coronary artery disease documented.

MedicationRequest._discrepancy: D1: primary-prevention aspirin only — safe to hold per high-bleed-risk protocol; confirm no secondary-prevention indication

code.text: Endoscopic submucosal dissection of gastric antral lesion (planned)

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Gastric antral mucosa: glandular adenoma with high-grade dysplasia. No definitive invasive carcinoma identified in sampled tissue.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Dombrowski. We're here today to plan the resection of that lesion in your stomach. Before we schedule the ESD, I want to go carefully through your medicines, because this is a procedure where bleeding is the main thing we manage.
MA: Before the doctor gets into it, let me get your vitals down — you're 5 foot 10, 185 pounds today. Temp 36.8, pulse 72, breathing 14, blood pressure 132 over 80, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Sure. Whatever you need to know.
DR: The chart shows you take a baby aspirin, 81 milligrams. Remind me why you were started on that — did you ever have a heart attack, a stent, or bypass surgery?
PT: No, nothing like that. My regular doctor put me on it a few years back, just as a precaution. For the heart, just in case. My heart's never given me trouble.
DR: That's an important distinction. Because it's only precautionary and you've never had heart disease, we can safely pause it before the procedure. If you'd had a stent I'd need to loop in cardiology first, but that's not your situation.
PT: Makes sense.
DR: Are you on any blood thinners?
PT: Yeah, actually — I'm on a blood thinner.
DR: Okay, tell me exactly what that is. What's the name, is it a prescription?
PT: Well... it's the fish oil. The omega-3. I take it every day. My neighbor said it thins the blood.
DR: Ah — good that you mentioned it, but that's a supplement, not a prescription blood thinner. It's not the same as warfarin or one of the newer anticoagulants. We'll still have you hold the fish oil for a bit beforehand, but it doesn't need special clearance.
PT: Oh. Okay, good.
DR: Anything else you take, even over the counter?
PT: I take ibuprofen for my knee most days. And the amlodipine every morning for my pressure.
DR: Both worth noting — the ibuprofen we'll hold, the blood-pressure pill you keep taking. Any drug allergies?
PT: None that I know of.
DR: Perfect. Let's get you scheduled and I'll send the exact hold instructions.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Dombrowski is a 69-year-old man presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation prior to endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) of a 25 mm gastric antral lesion showing high-grade dysplasia on biopsy (EGD 2026-06-02). Staging EUS (2026-06-23) demonstrated no submucosal invasion, favorable for en-bloc endoscopic resection. Visit focused on bleeding-risk and antithrombotic review for this high-bleed-risk resection.

Medication reconciliation: He takes low-dose aspirin 81 mg, which he confirms is for primary prevention only — he has never had myocardial infarction, coronary stent, or bypass, and it was started years ago by his PCP as a precaution. When asked about blood thinners he initially reported being 'on a blood thinner'; on clarification this is over-the-counter fish oil (omega-3), not a prescription anticoagulant. He additionally reports near-daily over-the-counter ibuprofen 400 mg for knee osteoarthritis (not previously documented) and amlodipine 5 mg each morning for hypertension (not on the chart medication list). No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 132/80, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 178 cm (5'10"), Wt 84 kg (185 lb), BMI 26.5. Pre-procedure labs (2026-07-08): hemoglobin 13.9 g/dL, platelets 244 x10^3/uL, INR 1.0, creatinine 0.98 mg/dL — all within acceptable range for endoscopic resection. Pathology (2026-06-05): gastric antral adenoma with high-grade dysplasia, no invasive carcinoma in sampled tissue.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Gastric antral high-grade dysplasia, no submucosal invasion on EUS — proceed with elective ESD (en-bloc resection).
- Antiplatelet: aspirin is primary prevention with no coronary artery disease — HOLD per high-bleed-risk resection protocol; no cardiology clearance required as there is no secondary-prevention indication.
- Reconcile chart: 'blood thinner' clarified as OTC fish oil (supplement, not anticoagulant) — hold pre-procedure; no anticoagulation bridging or anesthesia anticoag clearance needed.
- Add undocumented OTC ibuprofen — hold NSAID pre-procedure per bleeding-risk protocol.
- Add amlodipine 5 mg daily to medication list; continue, take morning of procedure with sip of water.
- Hypertension — continue home regimen.
- Labs acceptable (Hgb 13.9, plt 244, INR 1.0). Schedule ESD; nursing to send exact medication-hold and diet instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Planning your stomach procedure (ESD) to remove the abnormal area
• Which medicines to stop before the procedure so bleeding risk is lower
• Your aspirin, fish oil, ibuprofen, and blood-pressure pill

Next steps
• We will schedule your ESD; the team will send the exact dates and instructions.
• Stop your baby aspirin before the procedure — it is only a precaution for you and is safe to pause. You do NOT need a heart doctor's sign-off.
• Stop the fish oil and the ibuprofen before the procedure (the team will tell you exactly when).
• Keep taking your amlodipine for blood pressure, including the morning of the procedure with a small sip of water.
• We added the amlodipine and ibuprofen to your record and confirmed you have no known drug allergies.
• Your pre-procedure blood tests looked good.
• Call us with any questions before your procedure date.
